comments.author: @RoamPlanet-4K
comments.text: "The documentary claims Angkor collapsed because its water system failed under the stress of climate-driven droughts and mega-monsoons. But I believe the root cause was still human-induced. Centuries of massive deforestation for urban expansion and temple construction over 300 years are what altered the local microclimate and ruined the soil structure, making natural disasters far more devastating. Is it fair to blame nature, or was it the sheer hubris and greed of kings trying to re-engineer the environment?

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's a compelling counter-read — climate stress and centuries of human pressure on the land probably weren't separate forces so much as one accelerating the other. Where do you think the tipping point was: the deforestation itself, or the scale of construction it was feeding?

comments.author: @trueunrealglobe
comments.text: I appreciate that this channel doesn't rush through every location. Giving each destination enough time makes the entire experience feel much more immersive and memorable.

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Thank you — that pacing is a deliberate choice, and it's good to hear it lands the way we hope it does.

comments.author: @RobinFox-b5n
comments.text: I do wish that when it mentions some unusual feature or bird, it would actually show it......

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair note — matching the visual to the specific thing being named, rather than a general shot, is something worth tightening. Appreciate you pointing it out.
